sections.text: Functional and Mechanistic Explanation for the Unique Clinical Success of the Glucokinase Activator Dorzagliatin in the Treatment of Type 2 Diabetes

Glucokinase (GK) activators (GKAs) are a long-sought therapeutic modality for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. However, GKAs have failed in clinical trials, with the recent exception of dorzagliatin (Hua Medicine). A comprehensive approach using human islet perifusions, enzyme kinetics, X-ray crystallography, and modeling studies was applied to compare the effects of dorzagliatin with those of the unsuccessful GKA MK-0941 (Merck Pharmaceuticals), which is well characterized both clinically and mechanistically. Dorzagliatin improved glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in a dose- and glucose-dependent manner, in contrast to MK-0941, which induced maximal insulin secretion at low doses and glucose concentrations. To understand these functional differences, the atomic resolution structure of the dorzagliatin-GK complex was determined and compared with the GK–MK-0941 structure. MK-0941 bound to a pocket accessible in both open and closed conformations; had a strong interaction with Y214, the mutation of which produces the most clinically severe activating mutation; and produced a high energy barrier for the open-to-closed transition. In contrast, dorzagliatin only bound favorably to the closed form of GK, interacting primarily with R63 and causing a low energy barrier for the open-to-closed transition. This provides the molecular rationale for the clinical success of dorzagliatin, which can guide the future development of next-generation allosteric activators of GK.
Article Highlights
The type 2 diabetes (T2D) treatment dorzagliatin has achieved singular success among its drug class, known as glucokinase (GK) activators (GKAs).
A comprehensive approach using human islet perifusions, enzyme kinetics, X-ray crystallography, and modeling studies revealed the unique mechanism by which dorzagliatin activates GK.
Dorzagliatin dose-dependently reduces the glucose threshold for stimulation of insulin secretion and binds preferably to the closed form of GK, preventing overstimulation of the enzyme.
A renewed interest in GKAs, coupled with modern tools to assess their molecular interactions with GK, should guide the future development of novel GKA treatments for T2D.
Introduction

Type 2 diabetes (T2D) is a chronic condition characterized by elevated blood glucose levels, insulin resistance, and eventual loss of pancreatic β-cell function. The recent clinical introduction of glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists or dual agonists of the GLP-1 receptor and gastric inhibitory peptide (GIP) receptors has been a tremendous boon for patients with T2D. However, there is still a requirement for complementary glucose-lowering drugs, which are needed to enhance glycemic control in patients with T2D. Glucokinase (GK; i.e., hexokinase IV) plays a central role in glucose homeostasis; it is well positioned as a potential target for glucose-lowering drugs. A key enzyme parameter of pancreatic GK is S0.5, the glucose concentration at which it has 50% maximal activity, which determines the set point of blood glucose. For wild-type (WT) GK, S0.5 is ∼7 mmol/L, which maintains a preprandial blood glucose level of ∼5 mmol/L. An important property of GK is its positive cooperativity with increasing glucose concentration, producing a Hill coefficient of nearly 2. The resulting sigmoidal activity versus glucose concentration curve means GK is more sensitive to changes in blood glucose levels over the physiologic range than other hexokinases, making it well suited to act as the body’s glucostat. GK is cooperative despite being a monomer, and the mechanism of cooperativity is instrumental in targeting GK as a treatment for T2D. The enzyme undergoes a large glucose binding–driven conformational change from an open inactive conformation to a glucose-bound closed and active form. This conformational change primarily consists of a hinge-like motion between the large domain (residues 1–65 and 219–440) and the small domain (residues 66–218 and 441–461). On release of the products glucose-6-phosphate and ADP, GK relaxes slowly back to the open inactive form. At low glucose concentrations, there is time for GK to relax fully to the superopen inactive form. At increasing glucose concentrations, another glucose molecule is more likely to bind to GK before it fully relaxes, thus increasing the overall substrate-binding catalysis product release turnover rate. The direct relationship between the enzymatic properties of GK, blood glucose levels, and diabetes was reinforced by the discovery in the 1990s of point mutations of GK in patients with hyperglycemia resulting from GK loss of function or hypoglycemia resulting from GK gain of function. These mutations were linked with either maturity-onset diabetes of the young or hypoglycemia with congenital hyperinsulinism, respectively.

Based on the enzyme properties and known clinical mutations of GK, Matschinsky assessed the potential of GK activators (GKAs) in diabetes treatment. Notably, one of the most potently activated forms of GK is produced by a single mutation in Y214C with an activity index of 130. Residue 214 is located outside the active site of GK, in the hinge region between the large and small domains. The mutation shifts the conformational preference toward the closed active form. This suggested residue 214 could be targeted by small-molecule drugs to mimic the effect of the mutation, a strategy made more plausible by the presence of a natural surface pocket at residue 214. These GKAs could then be used to stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic β-cells and lower blood glucose levels in patients with T2D. Beginning in the 1990s, research and development by Hoffman-La Roche led to the discovery and characterization of multiple small-molecule GKAs. Soon after, many pharmaceutical companies followed suit and developed their own GKAs to treat T2D, including AstraZeneca (AZD-1650), Pfizer (PF-04991532), and Merck (MK-0941). Some of these GKAs proceeded to phase 2 clinical trials but were ultimately terminated because of serious adverse events, unfavorable safety profiles, or development of drug resistance. For example, MK-0941 caused multiple cases of severe hypoglycemia and fatty liver disease in trial participants with T2D, and even the lowest dose caused adverse events in >80% of trial participants. Because of these clinical failures during phase 2 trials, these companies halted their research efforts, and enthusiasm for GK as a drug target in T2D treatment diminished.

At present, there are high-resolution structures of more than 20 different activators in complex with GK. All these activators bind in the Y214-proximal pocket, and almost every GKA has the intended pharmacophore interaction of an aromatic ring stacked over the Y214 phenyl ring. However, the failure of these potent first-generation GKAs in the clinic raised the question of whether GK activation was a viable therapeutic strategy for T2D. Nevertheless, development of next-generation GKAs for T2D treatment was continued by a few companies, including vTv Therapeutics (TTP399), PegBio (PB-201), and Hua Medicine (HMS5552 [also known as dorzagliatin]).

In 2022, phase 3 clinical trials of dorzagliatin were successfully completed, and it was approved for treatment of T2D. Clinical studies showed that dorzagliatin produces a significant and durable reduction in HbA1c in patients with T2D, both in drug-naive settings and concomitantly with metformin, with minimal severe adverse events. The goal of this study was to elucidate the mechanisms underlying the unique clinical success of dorzagliatin over the well-characterized GKA MK-0941, which played a pivotal role in the course of GKA research and development efforts. This study may guide the rational development of better next-generation GKAs.
Research Design and Methods
Human Islets From Patients With T2D and Control Donors Without Diabetes

Control donors and donors with T2D were screened by the Integrated Islet Distribution Program, as previously described. Human islets were isolated from donor pancreata using standard multiorgan recovery and modified Ricordi techniques at multiple centers within the Integrated Islet Distribution Program network. We characterized the physiology and metabolic state of 51 human islet preparations isolated from 39 control donors and 12 donors with T2D. Publicly available data were also obtained through the Human Pancreas Analysis Program. Only donors aged >30 years were included in this study. Supplementary Table 1 lists donor information.
Perifusion of Human Islets

Three different protocols were used to assess hormone secretion in human islets: 1) the glucose step protocol, performed as previously described; 2) the glucose ramp protocol, where a fixed drug concentration was added before the glucose concentration was gradually increased from 0 to 16.7 mmol/L; and 3) the drug ramp protocol, where the glucose concentration remained fixed while the drug concentration was linearly increased from 0 to 3 μmol/L. Insulin was measured in perifusion aliquots, as previously described. All ramp studies were performed on the Bio-Rad system. For all step studies, the Biorep PERI4.1 perifusion system was used.
GK Enzyme Kinetic Assay

Human WT and mutated GK proteins were expressed in the Escherichia coli system with the glutathione S-transferase tag and purified with standard glutathione S-transferase protein purification. Enzymatic activity of GK proteins was determined with an NADP+/NADPH-coupled assay using glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase as the secondary reaction, as previously described. For all GK activity assays, 5 mmol/L ATP was used.
Dorzagliatin and GK Protein Cocrystallization

GK-apo crystals were produced using the hanging-drop vapor diffusion method at 18°C. A mixture of 1.5 μL GK protein solution (10 mg/mL in 20 mmol/L HEPES [pH 7.5], 100 mmol/L α-d-glucose, 50 mmol/L NaCl, 5% glycerol, and 1 mmol/L TCEP) and 1.0 μL reservoir solution was equilibrated against 500 μL reservoir solution. Microseeding was used to obtain high-quality GK-apo crystals in a reservoir buffer containing 100 mmol/L HEPES (pH 8.0) and 40% PEG 4000. To generate GK-dorzagliatin complex crystals, GK-apo crystals were transferred into the reservoir buffer supplemented with 5 mmol/L dorzagliatin and soaked for 2 h. For X-ray diffraction data collection, the crystals were cryoprotected by soaking in a solution of reservoir buffer supplemented with 20% ethylene glycol and subsequently flash frozen in liquid nitrogen. Diffraction data were collected at the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (Shanghai, China) on beamline BL02U1 at a wavelength of 0.97918 Å. Data processing was performed using XDS and AIMLESS. The structure of Protein Data Bank (PDB) entry 3IDH was used as a starting model for molecular replacement with Phaser. Iterative cycles of manual model building in Coot and refinement with Phenix.refine were carried out to finalize the structure. Ramachandran statistics were calculated using MolProbity.
Protein Docking and Analysis of Molecular Dynamics
GK structure models of 3IDH, 3H1V, and 3VEV were used for indication of mutation sites and compound docking analysis. Compound docking was analyzed using AutoDock 4.2.

Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations and metadynamic-enhanced sampling were performed using GROMACS 2023 software in conjunction with the PLUMED 2.9.0 plugin. Trajectory analysis and visualization were conducted using PyMOL 2.6.0. For force field selection, the AMBER ff14SB force field was applied to proteins, and the GAFF2 force field was used for the parametrization of small molecules. Preparatory steps for the simulation included the completion of missing residues in proteins and docking of small molecules using modeling and docking software, solvation with the TIP3P water model, and addition of an appropriate number of Na+ and Cl− ions to neutralize the total charge of the system via GROMACS solvate and genion commands. The system was initially subjected to energy minimization, followed by NVT and NPT equilibration simulations to ensure that the system reached equilibrium at 300 K and 1-bar pressure. Root mean square deviation (RMSD) was calculated on carbon α atoms for the selected loop residues for 500 ns: loop 1, residues from 41 to 71; loop 2, residues from 94 to 97; and loop 3, residues from 212 to 219.

During the 200-ns metadynamic simulation, we defined a collective variable of reaction coordinates based on the RMSD of minor structural domains to capture the transition of the protein from an open to closed state. The metadynamic method was used to enhance sampling efficiency, with a Gaussian potential well height of 1 kJ/mol, a width of 0.1 nm, and addition of a Gaussian function every 2,000 steps. The bias factor was set to 32 to intensify the degree of enhanced sampling.
Comparison of MK-0941 and Dorzagliatin Interactions With I159

Crystal structures of GK (PDB entry 1V4S) docking with MK-0941 and dorzagliatin were minimized to remove strain, and then, 2 ns of MD simulations were run at 300 K to equilibrate the structures, and 40 ns of MD simulations were run for analysis. Distances were monitored between the two side chain atoms of I159, CG1 and CG2, and the two closest heavy atoms of the drug: C2/C3 for MK-0941 and C10/C11 for dorzagliatin. To assess whether the simulations were converged, results from 20 and 40 ns of simulation were compared. The difference was within the precision of the measured distances.
Statistical Analyses
Statistical analyses were performed on GraphPad Prism software v9. Results are presented as mean ± SEM. Single time end point data were analyzed using single-factor ANOVA.
Data and Resource Availability

The data sets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on request. For details regarding human islet biosamples, please refer to the research resource identifiers (RRIDs) in Supplementary Table 1.
Results
Isolated Islets From Donors With T2D Have Impaired GSIS

The islets of 17 donors with T2D, compared with those of 31 control donors without diabetes (Supplementary Table 1 lists donor information), showed a marked reduction in insulin secretion in response to high-glucose and 3-isobutyl-1-methylxanthine, an inhibitor of cAMP phosphodiesterases (Fig. 1A). Amino acids and 3 mmol/L glucose produced similar stimulation of insulin secretion in control and T2D islets, as quantified by area under the curve analysis (Fig. 1B). In contrast, insulin secretion in response to 16.7 mmol/L glucose and 3-isobutyl-1-methylxanthine was significantly reduced in T2D islets compared with controls (P < 0.001 for both secretagogues) (Fig. 1C).

GKAs potentiate glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, which is deficient in T2D. A: Insulin secretion dynamics determined by perifusion of isolated human islets in donors with T2D compared with control donors. Mean ± SEM. B and C: Quantification of 3 and 16.7 mmol/L glucose-stimulated insulin secretion from the previous panel, calculated by area under the curve (AUC) analysis. Mean ± SEM. D and E: Changes in GSIS dynamics of T2D islets resulting from 1 μmol/L dorzagliatin or MK-0941. F and G: Quantification of 3 and 16.7 mmol/L GSIS from the previous panels, calculated by AUC analysis over entire duration of the intervention. AAM, amino acid metabolism; IBMX, 3-isobutyl-1-methylxanthine.
Effects of GKAs on Glucose-Stimulated Insulin Secretion in T2D Islets

The effect of GKAs on glucose-stimulated insulin secretion (GSIS) in T2D islets was tested using stepwise increases in glucose concentration. At 3 and 16.7 mmol/L glucose, 1 μmol/L dorzagliatin mildly potentiated insulin secretion (Fig. 1D). In contrast, 1 μmol/L MK-0941 maximally potentiated insulin secretion at 3 mmol/L glucose, but at 16.7 mmol/L glucose, it did not further stimulate insulin secretion (Fig. 1E). Area under the curve analysis (Fig. 1F and G) confirmed these observations. Loss of the high-glucose effect in MK-0941–treated islets did not seem to be due to exhaustion of insulin stores, because the KCl effect of MK-0941 was similar to that of untreated islets.
Dorzagliatin and MK-0941 Show Differential Dynamics of Potentiation of Insulin Secretion

The dose dependency of GKAs on insulin secretion in T2D islets was assessed by a drug ramp protocol, where the drug concentration gradually increased from 0 to 3 μmol/L in the presence of 3 mmol/L glucose. The dorzagliatin ramp induced a gradual dose-dependent increase in insulin secretion (Fig. 2A). In contrast, MK-0941 induced maximal stimulation of insulin secretion at nanomolar concentrations (Fig. 2B). Note that dorzagliatin-treated islets still responded to 16.7 mmol/L glucose, whereas MK-0941–treated islets were unable to do so.

Dorzagliatin, unlike MK-0941, dose-dependently potentiates GSIS in T2D islets. A and B: Insulin secretion dynamics in response to linear ramp of dorzagliatin or MK-0941 on background of 3 mmol/L glucose, followed by high glucose challenge. Mean ± SEM. C and D: Glucose dose dependency of insulin secretion from isolated T2D islets in presence of various concentrations of dorzagliatin or MK-0941. E and F: Glucose threshold of insulin secretion calculated from the previous panels, showing GKA dose dependency of glucose threshold in T2D islets. AAM, amino acid metabolism.
Dose-Dependent Effect of Dorzagliatin and All-or-None Effect of MK-0941 on Glucose Threshold of Insulin Secretion in T2D Islets

The effects of GKAs on the glucose threshold of insulin secretion in T2D islets were assessed by a glucose ramp protocol. Dorzagliatin produced a dose-dependent left shift in GSIS (Fig. 2C) and decrease in glucose threshold (Fig. 2F). In contrast, MK-0941 at all concentrations induced a maximal left shift in GSIS (Fig. 2D) and maximal decrease in glucose threshold (Fig. 2G), resulting in an all-or-none response.
Dorzagliatin and MK-0941 Have Differential GK Enzyme Activation Kinetics

The effects of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 on enzyme kinetics were tested using expressed and purified human GK protein. Drug dose dependency was determined at 3, 5, and 10 mmol/L glucose (Fig. 3A–C). Glucose dependency was determined at 0.025, 0.25, and 1 μmol/L drug (Fig. 3D–F). The EC50 of dorzagliatin remained unchanged when glucose increased from 3 to 5 mmol/L, but it decreased at 10 mmol/L glucose. In contrast, the EC50 of MK-0941 increased when glucose increased from 5 to 10 mmol/L. Dorzagliatin had higher Vmax of enzyme activation than MK-0941 regardless of glucose levels= (Fig. 3A–C). In a glucose-dependence assay, at the same compound concentrations, MK-0941 showed stronger activation of GK than dorzagliatin with lower glucose S0.5 (Supplementary Table 3). At concentrations of 0.025 (Fig. 3D) and 0.25 μmol/L (Fig. 3E), dorzagliatin had higher Vmax of activation than MK-0941; the difference diminished when compound concentration increased to 1 μmol/L (Fig. 3F). The EC50 difference of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 is summarized in Fig. 3G, clearly showing the different pattern of enzyme activation between the two compounds. Dorzagliatin showed a strong glucose dose-dependent activation pattern, whereas MK-0941 was a strong GK activator but lacked glucose dependence and had a lower Vmax compared with dorzagliatin.

Dorzagliatin and MK-0941 have differential GK activation kinetics. Recombinant WT human GKs were generated and expressed as GST fusion proteins in E. coli. Enzymatic activity of GK was determined with NADP+/NADPH-coupled assay using glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase as secondary reaction in 384-well plate assay format. A–C: Dose dependency of MK-0941 and dorzagliatin/HMS5552 activation of GK at 3, 5, and 10 mmol/L glucose. EC50 is defined as concentration of drug that produces half-maximal response. D–F: Glucose dependency of GK activity on background of 0.025, 0.25, and 1 μmol/L GKA, relative to DMSO. G: Comparison of EC50.
Cocrystallization of Dorzagliatin With GK

To understand the mechanism of action of dorzagliatin, purified GK was cocrystallized with dorzagliatin in the presence of glucose, which permitted dorzagliatin to bind to the closed conformation of GK. The structure was solved by X-ray crystallography to a resolution of 2.51 Å. As shown in Fig. 4A, dorzagliatin bound to the allosteric site of GK, as expected. Figure 4B shows key dorzagliatin interactions with several amino acid residues (Supplementary Fig. 1 shows alternative views). The interaction with R63 played an important role in dorzagliatin binding to GK. The carbonyl oxygen of R63 formed a hydrogen bond with hydrogen in an amido group of dorzagliatin. The amido group of R63 formed a hydrogen bond with the acceptor nitrogen in the pyrazole ring in dorzagliatin. MD simulations of dorzagliatin bound to GK were performed starting from the cocrystal structure (Supplementary Fig. 2A). Also, MD simulations of MK-0941 bound to GK were performed with a starting structure obtained by docking MK-0941 with structure 3H1V of GK (Fig. 4C and D and Supplementary Fig. 2B). As shown in Supplementary Fig. 2, several amino acid residues contributed significantly to the drug-protein interaction over the course of the 500-ns MD simulations. Using a residue energy decomposition scheme, as described in Research Design and Methods, we found that R63 provided the strongest contribution to the dorzagliatin-GK interaction. In contrast, for the MK-0941–GK complex, residue Y214 made the strongest drug-protein interaction. Note that Y214 is a key residue, because the activating mutation Y214C causes severe clinical hypoglycemia, and that all published cocrystallizations of GK with all other activators show contact with Y214 (Supplementary Table 2). Analysis of the hydrogen bonds between drug and GK during the MD simulations showed that a hydrogen bond between the carbonyl oxygen of R63 (acceptor) and hydrogen 25 of dorzagliatin (donor) was present in 95% of the 500-ns MD simulations. In contrast, the hydrogen bond formed between GK R63 and MK-0941 showed up only 30% of the time in the MD simulations of the MK-0941–GK complex (Supplementary Table 4).

X-ray crystallography shows differential binding of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 to GK. A: X-ray crystal structure of GK-dorzagliatin binding site. Large domain is colored in salmon, and the small domain is in gray. Inset highlights interactions between dorzagliatin and select residues of GK. B: Map of dorzagliatin-residue interactions (PyMOL v2020). Color annotations described on at bottom of panel. C: Binding site of GK–MK-0941 complex (docking of MK-0941 with GK, PDB 3H1V), for comparison with panel A. D: Map of MK-0941–residue interactions, for comparison with panel B.

To test if R63 is a key residue for the binding of dorzagliatin, several mutated GK proteins with different amino acid substitutions were prepared, including R63W, R63E, R63D, and R63G. R63W had the same enzyme kinetics as WT GK, whereas R63D, R63E, and R63G substitutions of GK showed a left-shifted curve, suggested gain of function (Supplementary Fig. 3A). The R63D mutation lost almost all response to dorzagliatin activation (EC50 increased ∼50-fold; Vmax decreased by ∼90%) but remained partially responsive to MK-0941 stimulation (EC50 increased only ∼7.5-fold; Vmax decreased by ∼60%) (Supplementary Fig. 3B and C). The other three mutations, R63E, R63G, and R63W, produced a substantial reduction in responsiveness to both dorzagliatin and MK-0941 activation.
Differential Binding of Dorzagliatin and MK-0941 Based on Metadynamics

Drug-GK binding affinity was also calculated from the MD simulations. Compared with dorzagliatin, MK-0941 has a more negative free energy of binding (e.g., higher affinity with GK) (Table 1). We also used metadynamics to simulate the transition of GK from open to closed form in the presence of glucose. When GK was not bound to any activator, the stable structure was the open inactive form, because it had a lower free energy than the closed form (Fig. 5A and D and Supplementary Video 1). When bound to dorzagliatin, GK could freely switch between open and closed forms, with a small energy barrier for transition (Fig. 5B and E and Supplementary Video 2). In contrast, when bound to MK-0941, GK was forced into the closed form, and a high energy barrier prevented transition back to the open form (Fig. 5C and F and Supplementary Video 3).
Predicted binding free energies and individual energy terms of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 to WT GK

*van der Waals contribution.
†Electrostatic contribution.
‡Polar contribution of desolvation.
§Nonpolar contribution of desolvation.
ǁPredicted binding free energy.

MD simulations with metadynamics-enhanced sampling provide implications for differential binding. A–C: MD simulation with metadynamics-enhanced sampling showing transition from open to closed form of GK alone, bound to dorzagliatin, or bound to MK-0941, respectively. D–F: Predicted free energy landscape of GK alone, bound to dorzagliatin, or bound to MK-0941, respectively.
Structural Analysis of GK Crystal Structures

Thirty-five structures of GK determined by X-ray crystallography were obtained from the PDB database. Based on the RMSD of Cα atoms with respect to structure 1V4S, 27 of these GK structures were classified as closed, six were classified as open, and two were classified as superopen. One structure, 4LC9, was a complex with the GK regulatory protein (GKRP). Of the other structures, 29 were complexes with 25 different first-generation GKAs. Structure 1V4S was one of three structures docking with MK-0941. In all 29 activator-GK complexes, residues V62, R63, and Y214 were in contact with the activator, whereas 25 of the complexes also had contact residues M210 and I211 in common, showing that the activator binding locations were essentially the same. This structural analysis is summarized in Supplementary Table 2.
Comparison of Dorzagliatin and MK-0941 Interactions With I159

In addition to residues R63 and Y214, residue I159 plays a role in differential binding of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 to GK. MD simulations were run on the GK-dorzagliatin crystal structure and GK structure 1V4S docking to MK-0941. In addition, the loop consisting of residues 154–179 was also monitored. This loop contained the I159 residue at the turn and was structured only in the closed form (Fig. 6A and B). The average distance to I159 was 4.8 ± 0.5 Å for dorzagliatin and 5.3 ± 0.7 Å for MK-0941. In the dorzagliatin-GK complex, the 154–179 loop had an RMSD of 1.2 ± 0.3 Å, compared with 2.0 ± 0.4 Å for the MK-0941–GK complex. The lesser distance of dorzagliatin to residue I159 and the smaller movement of the 154–179 loop suggested a stronger interaction of this key segment of GK with dorzagliatin.

Structural comparisons of GK and its interactions with dorzagliatin and GKRP. A and B: Comparison of superopen (A) and closed (B) structures of GK. Residues between H156 and N180 are missing in open form, but in closed form, they are structured into loop-strand-turn-strand structure, indicated in orange in panel B. C: Crystal structure of dorzagliatin bound to GK. Key hydrophobic interaction of C10, C11 atoms of drug and residue I159 of GK at tip of the turn is depicted. D: Structure of GKRP in complex with open form of GK. Note residue Y214, highlighted in orange, is accessible, whereas the 158–179 loop is disordered, and residues 465–467 of GKRP occlude the site where I159 would be in closed form. Note that MK-0941 primarily contacts residue Y214 when bound to GK, whereas dorzagliatin primarily binds residues R63 and I159. E: Structure of GKRP in complex with GK, highlighting residues R63 and Y214.

The liver isoform of GK has distinct but near-identical sequences and structure to pancreatic GK; however, liver GK is regulated by GKRP, which binds GK and locks it in the superopen inactive conformation. Therefore, the residues R63 and Y214 and the 154–179 loop were also assessed in GK complexed with GKRP. When GKRP was bound, the 154–179 loop was disordered, and residues 465–467 of GKRP occluded R63 of GK (Fig. 6D and E). In contrast, the Y214 pocket was not covered by GKRP (Fig. 6D and E).
Discussion

T2D is characterized by a functional impairment of pancreatic β-cells and loss of glucose sensing. GK plays a central role in glucose sensing in β-cells; therefore, GKAs have been tested in clinical trials to reduce hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes by augmenting insulin secretion. However, evaluation of the previous generation of GKAs, including MK-0941, was terminated in clinical trials because of hypoglycemia in patients with T2D. This study using isolated human islets shows that MK-0941 produces an all-or-none effect on GSIS, whereas dorzagliatin dose-dependently affected the glucose threshold of GSIS. This may explain the success of dorzagliatin in improving HbA1c without causing hypoglycemia in clinical trials of patients with T2D.

To understand the clinical success of dorzagliatin compared with other GKAs, we applied a multipronged approach, including functional tests, enzyme kinetics, structural biology, and MD. In contrast to the rapid spike in insulin secretion resulting from MK-0941, dorzagliatin gradually potentiated GSIS in isolated T2D human islets. In addition, dorzagliatin preserved insulin secretory responses to high glucose, whereas these responses were lost in MK-0941–treated islets. These functional differences are explained by the molecular interactions between GK, GKA, and glucose. The experimental structure of dorzagliatin bound to GK allowed us to rationalize the different pharmacologic profiles of dorzagliatin and MK-0941 seen in the islet cell experiments. MK-0941 could bind to the open form of GK prevalent at low glucose, because the Y214-proximal binding pocket exists in both closed and open conformations. Thus, MK-0941 potently activated GK and stimulated almost maximal insulin release at low glucose levels. This overstimulation was exacerbated by the larger energy barrier between open and closed forms with MK-0941 bound, which acted to lock GK in the active state and prevent gradual GK activation. Additionally, all published cocrystallizations of GK with allosteric activators have shown contact at Y214, suggesting that all other GKAs may share the same mode of action. Dorzagliatin, in contrast, primarily hydrogen bonded with residue R63 (present in 95% of the MD simulations) and interacted with residue I159, which was well positioned in the closed form (Fig. 6A). Thus, dorzagliatin preferred to bind the closed form of GK, which was more prevalent when glucose was high. Additionally, the dorzagliatin-GK complex had a low energy barrier for transition between open and closed states. Our inference regarding allosteric mechanism is that MK-0941 acts by the induced conformation pathway; that is, it binds first to the open conformation in the pocket containing Y214 and then induces the change to the closed form. Dorzagliatin, in contrast, acts by the conformational capture (selection) pathway; it can only bind well to the closed form, but once bound, it stabilizes this active state. Therefore, dorzagliatin acts synergistically with glucose to promote insulin secretion.

Our modeling results may also explain why dorzagliatin, a dual activator of GK in the pancreas and liver, preferentially activates pancreatic GK and not liver GK, avoiding adverse effects such as hepatolipidosis, hyperlipidemia, and severe hypoglycemia. Our results indicate that GKRP occludes GK residue R63 and disorders the 154–179 loop, which is important for dorzagliatin binding (Fig. 6D and E). This may suggest that when liver GK is bound to GKRP, dorzagliatin would not be able to access and activate GK. In contrast, the Y214 pocket was not covered by GKRP, so MK-0941 and other first-generation activators could always bind liver GK, bypassing GKRP and constitutively activating the enzyme. Liver GK was activated when hepatic glucose levels were >10 mmol/L, which caused GKRP to dissociate from GK. With GKRP absent, dorzagliatin could bind and further activate liver GK, helping promote the removal of postprandial glucose when glucose levels were high. The high-affinity binding of MK-0941 locked GK in the closed conformation, so GKRP could not bind to GK even when glucose levels decreased. In contrast, the lower-affinity binding of dorzagliatin allowed it to release from GK, so GKRP could rebind GK when glucose levels were low.

In conclusion, the mechanism by which dorzagliatin activates GK is fundamentally different from that of MK-0941 and all other failed activators, which show similar binding characteristics to MK-0941. This would explain the clinical success of dorzagliatin compared with all other GK activators tested thus far. This study validates the concept of GK as a drug target in T2D treatment. As a complement to GLP-1/GIP and sodium–glucose cotransporter 2 drugs, which are highly effective in patients with diabetes with obesity and cardiovascular complications, GK activators are a promising class of glucose-lowering drug, which may be useful for patients with diabetes with defects in glucose sensing and metabolism. However, GK allosteric activators must exhibit the glucose dependency necessary to potentiate insulin secretion while avoiding hypoglycemia. Therefore, a renewed interest in GK activators, coupled with modern tools to assess their molecular interactions with GK, should guide the future development of novel GK activator medicines for T2D.
